summary:
The Battle of Manila Bay on 1 May 1898 was the opening engagement of the [[event:2551|Spanish-American War]] in the Pacific and one of the most lopsided naval victories in modern history. Commodore George Dewey's Asiatic Squadron of seven warships destroyed the entire Spanish Pacific fleet of ten vessels in a matter of hours without losing a single American sailor in combat.

Dewey had been alerted before the war began by Assistant Secretary of the Navy Theodore Roosevelt (acting during his superior's absence) to prepare for offensive operations against the Spanish Philippines. When war was declared in April 1898, Dewey immediately sailed from Hong Kong to Manila Bay, arriving before dawn on 1 May. He famously issued the order to his fleet captain aboard the Olympia: 'You may fire when you are ready, Gridley.'

The Spanish fleet under Rear Admiral Patricio Montojo had anchored near the Cavite naval station. Spanish gunnery was poor and their ships were antiquated. The American ships made five passes along the Spanish line, their accurate gunnery destroying the Spanish vessels systematically. By noon all ten Spanish ships had been sunk or burned. Spanish casualties numbered 77 killed and 271 wounded; the Americans suffered no deaths in combat (eight men died of heat stroke during the battle).

The battle destroyed Spanish naval power in the Pacific and enabled the subsequent American capture of Manila with the assistance of Filipino independence fighters led by Emilio Aguinaldo. However, the acquisition of the Philippines created the dilemma at the heart of American imperialism: what to do with eight million Filipinos who wanted independence rather than a new colonial master. The Philippine-American War that followed cost far more American lives than the Spanish-American War itself.
